**Associazione Sportiva Reggina 1914**, commonly referred to as **Reggina**, is an [Italian football](/wiki/Football_in_Italy "Football in Italy") club based in [Reggio Calabria](/wiki/Reggio_Calabria "Reggio Calabria"). They play their home matches at the 27,763-seater [Stadio Oreste Granillo](/wiki/Stadio_Oreste_Granillo "Stadio Oreste Granillo") and are currently playing in the [Serie D](/wiki/Serie_D "Serie D").

They are nicknamed the *Amaranto* (amaranth) after their official dark red colour. The club was formerly known as **Reggina Calcio** before declaring bankruptcy in 2015, as well as **A.S.D. Reggio Calabria** in the 2015–16 season, **Urbs Reggina 1914** from 2016 to 2019, **Reggina 1914** from 2019 to 2023, and **LFA Reggio Calabria** in the 2023–24 season.

In its previous guise, the club played in the [Serie A](/wiki/Serie_A "Serie A") for nine seasons between 1999 and 2009, including a seven-year consecutive run from 2002. During these years, the club narrowly avoided relegation in most seasons and never finished in the top half of the table. After failing to return in the first few years, the club fell into financial and sporting difficulties, culminating in relegation to the lower tiers of Italian football before returning to Serie B in 2020.

## Names

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=AS_Reggina_1914&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: Names")]

The club was founded on 11 January 1914 as **Unione Sportiva Reggio Calabria**. It changed name many times (**Società Calcistica Reggio**, **Reggio Foot Ball Club**, **Associazione Sportiva Reggina**, **Società Sportiva La Dominante**), finally assuming the denomination **Reggina Calcio** in 1986.

After the 2015 bankruptcy, the club had used **A.S.D. Reggio Calabria** as the new name of the new legal person, and then the club changed to the denomination **Urbs Reggina 1914 [S.r.l](/wiki/Societ%C3%A0_a_responsabilit%C3%A0_limitata "Società a responsabilità limitata").** in 2016, and, between 2019 and 2023, **Reggina 1914 S.r.l.**.

Following the most recent club exclusion in 2023, the club was refounded as **LFA Reggio Calabria**.[[2]](#cite_note-2)

## History

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=AS_Reggina_1914&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: History")]

|  |  |
| --- | --- |
|  | This article **appears to be [slanted towards recent events](/wiki/Wikipedia:Recentism "Wikipedia:Recentism").** Please try to keep recent events in historical perspective and [add more content](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=AS_Reggina_1914&action=edit) related to non-recent events. *(May 2019)* *([Learn how and when to remove this message](/wiki/Help:Maintenance_template_removal "Help:Maintenance template removal"))* |

The club was founded on 11 January 1914.

In 1986, Pasquale "Lillo" Foti became [managing director](/wiki/Managing_director "Managing director") of the newly re-formed and renamed Reggina Calcio. In 1991, he became president.

### Serie A (1999–2009)

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=AS_Reggina_1914&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: Serie A (1999–2009)")]

From the late 1990s to the 2000s, Reggina has been alternating between the top two levels of the Italian league system. They reached the top division of Italian football, [Serie A](/wiki/Serie_A "Serie A"), for the first time in 1999.[[3]](#cite_note-3) [Two years later](/wiki/2000%E2%80%9301_Serie_A "2000–01 Serie A"), they lost a relegation playout to [Verona](/wiki/Hellas_Verona_F.C. "Hellas Verona F.C."), being consequently relegated to [2001–02 Serie B](/wiki/2001%E2%80%9302_Serie_B "2001–02 Serie B").[[4]](#cite_note-4) Reggina finished third in Serie B in 2002, earning a return to Serie A. In [2003](/wiki/2002%E2%80%9303_Serie_A "2002–03 Serie A"), Reggina survived a relegation playout against [Atalanta](/wiki/Atalanta_B.C. "Atalanta B.C."). They would spend the next 7 years maintaining their Serie A status until their eventual relegation in the 2008–09 season.

In August 2006, they were indicted as part of the second wave of *[Calciopoli](/wiki/Calciopoli "Calciopoli")* investigations. Originally punished with a 15-point deduction for the [2006–07 Serie A](/wiki/2006%E2%80%9307_Serie_A "2006–07 Serie A"),[[5]](#cite_note-5) then reduced to 11 points following appeal.[[6]](#cite_note-6) Despite the heavy deduction of points, Reggina managed to save themselves from relegation, defeating newly crowned [UEFA Champions League](/wiki/2006%E2%80%9307_UEFA_Champions_League "2006–07 UEFA Champions League") winners [Milan](/wiki/A.C._Milan "A.C. Milan") on the final matchday and ending the season with 40 points (including the deduction), just one single point above the third relegation spot, occupied by [Chievo](/wiki/A.C._ChievoVerona "A.C. ChievoVerona"). They poorly started the [Serie A 2007-08](/wiki/Serie_A_2007-08 "Serie A 2007-08"), causing head coach [Massimo Ficcadenti](/wiki/Massimo_Ficcadenti "Massimo Ficcadenti") to be sacked and replaced by [Renzo Ulivieri](/wiki/Renzo_Ulivieri "Renzo Ulivieri").[[7]](#cite_note-ulivieri_reggina-7) A third managerial change, with Ulivieri fired and replacing with team scout [Nevio Orlandi](/wiki/Nevio_Orlandi "Nevio Orlandi"), proved to be successful as Reggina improved their results and performances, escaping relegation with key wins at Catania, and home to Empoli. Orlandi was subsequently confirmed at the helm of the *amaranto* for the [Serie A 2008–09](/wiki/Serie_A_2008%E2%80%9309 "Serie A 2008–09").

### Serie B (2009–2014)

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=AS_Reggina_1914&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: Serie B (2009–2014)")]

Since their relegation in the 2008–09 season, Reggina has become slightly inconsistent[*[according to whom?](/wiki/Wikipedia:Manual_of_Style/Words_to_watch#Unsupported_attributions "Wikipedia:Manual of Style/Words to watch")*] in their attempts to return to Italy's top flight. The 2009–10 season would see three coaches at the helm: [Walter Novellino](/wiki/Walter_Novellino "Walter Novellino"), [Ivo Iaconi](/wiki/Ivo_Iaconi "Ivo Iaconi"), [Roberto Breda](/wiki/Roberto_Breda "Roberto Breda"). Despite possessing Bonazzoli, Carmona, Tedesco, Brienza, and homegrown star Missiroli, they were unable to improve on 13th.[[8]](#cite_note-8) Disappointing for a team just relegated from the top division. The top goalscorer for the campaign was [Franco Brienza](/wiki/Franco_Brienza "Franco Brienza") with 12 goals.

The 2010–11 season was regarded as one of the Amaranto's best in Serie B. Shockingly they would conduct their usual coaching merry-go-round, as [Gianluca Atzori](/wiki/Gianluca_Atzori "Gianluca Atzori") would lead them to a 6th-place finish and playoffs to Serie A.[[9]](#cite_note-football-lineups.com-9) they would stumble at the last hurdle losing to Novara in a two-legged playoff. Top players include Acerbi, Missiroli, Tedesco, Brienza, Bonazzoli (C) and [Milan](/wiki/Milan "Milan") Loanee Adiyiah. Top goal scorer: Bonazzoli with 19 goals[[9]](#cite_note-football-lineups.com-9)

The 2011–12 season was another disappointing season for the Amaranto, with a 12th-placed finish. Two coaches took charge of Reggina this season: [Roberto Breda](/wiki/Roberto_Breda "Roberto Breda") initially, before being sacked and replaced by [Angelo Gregucci](/wiki/Angelo_Gregucci "Angelo Gregucci"), only to be replaced by Breda again towards the end of the season. Unlike the previous season, they did not make the play-offs.[[10]](#cite_note-10)

The 2012–13 season would be marred by yet another controversy similar to that of 2006. Reggina were penalized for the latest match-fixing scandal that hit Italian shores and were given a −4 penalty as a result.[[11]](#cite_note-11) After appeal it was reduced to −2 instead. They were in contention for playoff places right up to the last few rounds, but poor form saw them finish 11th.

The 2013–14 season ended in disaster, as Reggina won just six out of 42 games and finished second bottom, resulting in relegation to [Lega Pro](/wiki/Lega_Pro "Lega Pro"). The season also marked Foti's retirement from his role as president, with Giuseppe Ranieri taking over.

### Lega Pro

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=AS_Reggina_1914&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: Lega Pro")]

For the club's [2014–15 Lega Pro](/wiki/2014%E2%80%9315_Lega_Pro "2014–15 Lega Pro") campaign, Reggina began the season with former captain [Francesco Cozza](/wiki/Francesco_Cozza_(footballer) "Francesco Cozza (footballer)") as head coach. After a difficult start to the season and two coaching changes, [youth team](/wiki/Campionato_Nazionale_Dante_Berretti "Campionato Nazionale Dante Berretti") coach and former player [Giacomo Tedesco](/wiki/Giacomo_Tedesco "Giacomo Tedesco") was hired as head coach for the final three weeks of the season. Despite winning two of the final three matches, Reggina finished last in the league and would have to rely on an appeal of their point penalty to lift them out of the relegation zone. The appeal was successful, and 2 points were returned, moving them out of last place. Tedesco guided the team to survival in the playoff over rivals Messina.

### Serie D one-year stint

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=AS_Reggina_1914&action=edit&section=6 "Edit section: Serie D one-year stint")]

Despite avoiding relegation in the 2014–15 season, Reggina failed to meet the deadline to register for Lega Pro and the club declared bankruptcy.[[12]](#cite_note-12) A new legal person of the club, "A.S.D. Reggio Calabria", was formed to play in Serie D for the 2015–16 season,[[13]](#cite_note-13) Reggio Calabria ended the season in 4th place, losing in the first round of playoffs against [Cavese](/wiki/Cavese "Cavese"). During the season, the club also re-incorporated from *associazione sportiva dilettantistica* to *società sportiva dilettantistica a responsabilità limitata* [legal form](/wiki/Legal_form "Legal form").

Reggio Calabria was owned by Mimmo Praticò,[[14]](#cite_note-14) former regional president of [CONI](/wiki/Italian_National_Olympic_Committee "Italian National Olympic Committee").[[15]](#cite_note-15)

### Back to Serie C

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=AS_Reggina_1914&action=edit&section=7 "Edit section: Back to Serie C")]

In June 2016, it was reported that the club was renamed from "S.S.D. Reggio Calabria a r.l." to "S.S.D. Urbs Sportiva Reggina 1914 a r.l.".[[16]](#cite_note-16) The club was then renamed as Urbs Reggina 1914 [S.r.l](/wiki/Societ%C3%A0_a_responsabilit%C3%A0_limitata "Società a responsabilità limitata").

Despite finishing as the losing side in the first round of the promotion playoffs of [2015–16 Serie D](/wiki/2015%E2%80%9316_Serie_D "2015–16 Serie D"), the club filed for Lega Pro (later renamed Serie C) repechage to fill one of the vacancies for the 2016–17 season[[17]](#cite_note-17) and was successfully admitted.[[18]](#cite_note-18) Reggina ended the season in 13th place.

In January 2019, facing a crisis with a potential player strike due to non-payment of salaries,[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*] the club was sold to Italian entrepreneur Luca Gallo.[[19]](#cite_note-19)

On 1 July 2019, the club announced a change in name to "Reggina 1914 S.r.l.".[[20]](#cite_note-20)

### Serie B return

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=AS_Reggina_1914&action=edit&section=8 "Edit section: Serie B return")]

Reggina won its Serie C group in [2020](/wiki/2019%E2%80%9320_Serie_C "2019–20 Serie C"), earning a promotion back to the [Serie B](/wiki/Serie_B "Serie B") for the upcoming season. The team, upon its Serie B return, included experienced players from higher levels, including [Jérémy Ménez](/wiki/J%C3%A9r%C3%A9my_M%C3%A9nez "Jérémy Ménez") and [Germán Denis](/wiki/Germ%C3%A1n_Denis "Germán Denis").

In the [2022-23](/wiki/2023%E2%80%9324_Serie_B "2023–24 Serie B") season under the guidance of manager [Filippo Inzaghi](/wiki/Filippo_Inzaghi "Filippo Inzaghi"), Reggina finished in a playoff position, despite suffering a five-point deduction for failing to pay taxes and player salaries on time. The deduction was initially seven points, but was reduced to five following an appeal.

### 2023 exclusion and restart

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=AS_Reggina_1914&action=edit&section=9 "Edit section: 2023 exclusion and restart")]

On 1 July 2023, Reggina was excluded from Serie B for the [2023-24](/wiki/2023%E2%80%9324_Serie_B "2023–24 Serie B") season after Covisoc rejected their application due to financial irregularities.[[21]](#cite_note-21) On appeal, the Federal Council confirmed Reggina's exclusion. At the next level of appeal, the *Collegio di Garanzia* of the [Italian Olympic Committee](/wiki/Italian_Olympic_Committee "Italian Olympic Committee") withheld the decision. On 3 August, the Administrative Court of Rome again rejected Reggina's request for readmission. On 30 August, Reggina was again rejected at the final level of appeals, the [Council of State](/wiki/Council_of_State_(Italy) "Council of State (Italy)"). Brescia was readmitted to the league after Reggina's confirmed exclusion.

On 1 September 2023, acting mayor of the Metropolitan City of Reggio Calabria, Paolo Brunetti, received approval from the [Italian Football Federation](/wiki/Italian_Football_Federation "Italian Football Federation") to enroll a club in [Serie D](/wiki/Serie_D "Serie D") for the 2023–24 season. The club was successively handed over to a Catania-based consortium led by Antonino Ballarino, named **La Fenice Amaranto** (The amaranth phoenix), which submitted a league application and will take part to the season under the new denomination of **LFA Reggio Calabria**, as the new club was forbidden to use the Reggina 1914 name or logo for the upcoming season.[*[why?](/wiki/Wikipedia:Please_clarify "Wikipedia:Please clarify")*]

On 29 May 2024, Ballarino and LFA Reggio Calabria acquired the rights to the name and history of Reggina; the club was formally renamed to **AS Reggina 1914** on 18 July 2024.[[1]](#cite_note-name2024-1)

## Rivalries

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=AS_Reggina_1914&action=edit&section=11 "Edit section: Rivalries")]

Reggina are fierce rivals with neighbours [Messina](/wiki/A.C.R._Messina "A.C.R. Messina"), who are just a twenty-minute ferry ride apart from each other. Twice every season, they clash in the *[Derby dello Stretto](/wiki/Derby_dello_Stretto "Derby dello Stretto")* ([Strait of Messina](/wiki/Strait_of_Messina "Strait of Messina") Derby). In the 2014–15 season, Reggina defeated Messina in both legs of the playout to send Messina down to Serie D. There is also a major Calabrian derby between Reggina and [Crotone](/wiki/F.C._Crotone "F.C. Crotone"), but both teams have not played in the same league in for two years. Reggina also has a derby with fellow Calabrian team [Catanzaro](/wiki/U.S._Catanzaro_1929 "U.S. Catanzaro 1929"), which is currently in Serie B.

## Colours and badges

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=AS_Reggina_1914&action=edit&section=12 "Edit section: Colours and badges")]

Reggina used a logo with a letter R and a [football](/wiki/Ball_(association_football) "Ball (association football)") on it. Some variant of the logo, had 1986, the year of foundation of Reggina Calcio on it.[[22]](#cite_note-22) Due to 2015 bankruptcy, the new owner introduced a new logo as the old design was tied to the old legal person of the club. Due to 2016 renaming, some modification was introduced, which a letter R was re-introduced in the logo.[[23]](#cite_note-2016logo-23) However, the design of the ball on the 2016 logo was different from the historical logo, which the 2016 logo used a leather ball.[[23]](#cite_note-2016logo-23) In 2019, the club reverted to use the historical design of the logo.[[24]](#cite_note-24)
